findings.description: day_range builds a fully-materialized list of one date object per day with no upper bound on (end - start), so attacker-controlled dates (e.g. start=0001-01-01, end=9999-12-31 => ~3.65M elements) cause unbounded memory/CPU allocation — a remote resource-exhaustion DoS (CWE-770 / CWE-400); cap the span or stream via a generator.

business_days_between is a second public entry point that calls day_range(start, end) and then iterates the entire materialized list, inheriting and amplifying the same unbounded-range DoS (CWE-400) for attacker-supplied start/end dates.

end_of_day_exclusive on date.max (9999-12-31) does start_of_day(d) + timedelta(days=1), which raises OverflowError (not the ValueError contract callers expect), turning a boundary date input into an unhandled exception / 500 — minor availability/robustness issue (CWE-755).

parse_iso_date accepts an unbounded-length attacker-controlled string into strptime with no length guard; not a ReDoS (the %Y-%m-%d format is bounded, non-backtracking) and low-impact, but worth a defensive max-length check at the request boundary since this is a public parse entry point.
